1: ἐὰν καὶ προλημφθῇ ἄνθρωπός τινι παραπτώματι (if someone is caught/overtaken in some trespass, G4258) — prolambhanō may mean "caught in the act" or "overtaken, surprised." ὑμεῖς οἱ πνευματικοί (you the spiritual ones) — the adjective designates those who live by the Spirit (5:25); their responsibility for restoration is grounded in their Spirit-life. ἐν πνεύματι πρᾳΰτητος (in a spirit of gentleness) — the spirit/Spirit of gentleness: the same gentle quality named as a fruit of the Spirit (5:23).
2: Ἀλλήλων τὰ βάρη βαστάζετε (bear one another's burdens, G941) — the present imperative: an ongoing practice. καὶ οὕτως ἀναπληρώσετε τὸν νόμον τοῦ Χριστοῦ (and in this way you will fulfill the law of Christ, G3551) — the future anaplērōsete (will fulfill, G378) has a different prefix from 5:14's peplērōtai; ana-plēroō means to fill up completely. The "law of Christ" (G3551 here in a positive sense) is not a new legal code but the governing principle of Christ's own self-giving (cf. 5:14: love your neighbor), described as "law" because it functions as the normative standard.
3: εἰ γὰρ δοκεῖ τις εἶναί τι μηδὲν ὤν (for if anyone thinks they are something when they are nothing) — the self-deception here is the inflation of self-estimation that enables judgment of others (v.1's "you who are spiritual") while exempting oneself. The present participle ōn (being nothing) is the corrective reality check.
4: τὸ δὲ ἑαυτοῦ ἔργον δοκιμαζέτω ἕκαστος (let each one examine/test their own work, G1381) — dokimazō is the test-and-approve verb; the examination is self-directed and specific to one's own ergon (work/action). εἰς ἑαυτὸν μόνον τὸ καύχημα ἕξει καὶ οὐκ εἰς τὸν ἕτερον (they will have ground for boasting in themselves alone and not in regard to another) — boasting grounded in comparative evaluation of others is eliminated; whatever ground for confidence exists is internal and self-referential.
5: ἕκαστος γὰρ τὸ ἴδιον φορτίον βαστάσει (for each one will carry their own load) — the different word from v.2's barē (heavy burdens): phortion (G5413) is the pack or standard load, not an exceptional weight. There is no contradiction with v.2; each person has their own appropriate individual responsibility before God, while the community bears each other's excessive burdens.
6: κοινωνείτω δὲ ὁ κατηχούμενος τὸν λόγον τῷ κατηχοῦντι ἐν πᾶσιν ἀγαθοῖς (let the one being instructed in the word share all good things with the one instructing, G2841) — the koinōneō verb indicates material sharing; the student-teacher relationship incurs a practical obligation of support. The logos (word) that is taught is the gospel.
7: μὴ πλανᾶσθε· θεὸς οὐ μυκτηρίζεται (do not be deceived: God is not mocked, G3456) — mykterizō (G3456) literally means to turn up one's nose at someone in contempt; God cannot be treated with contemptuous disregard. ὃ γὰρ ἐὰν σπείρῃ ἄνθρωπος, τοῦτο καὶ θερίσει (for whatever a person sows, that is also what they will reap) — the agricultural axiom of cosmic accountability: the harvest corresponds to the seed.
8: ὁ σπείρων εἰς τὴν σάρκα ἑαυτοῦ ἐκ τῆς σαρκὸς θερίσει φθοράν (the one sowing to their own flesh will reap corruption from the flesh, G4561 disp=4) — sarx here in its negative Pauline sense: the fallen human self-orientation. Sowing to the flesh is living to please the flesh-principle; the harvest is phthora (G5356, corruption/decay), the ultimate outcome of the Adamic existence. ὁ δὲ σπείρων εἰς τὸ πνεῦμα ἐκ τοῦ πνεύματος θερίσει ζωὴν αἰώνιον (but the one sowing to the Spirit will reap eternal life from the Spirit) — the harvest of Spirit-oriented living is zōē aiōnios (life of the age to come): the eschatological life that the Spirit is the firstfruits of.
9: τὸ δὲ καλὸν ποιοῦντες μὴ ἐγκακῶμεν (in doing good let us not grow weary, G1573) — the present subjunctive hortatory: an ongoing encouragement to persevere. καιρῷ γὰρ ἰδίῳ θερίσομεν μὴ ἐκλυόμενοι (for in its own time we will reap if we do not give up) — kairo idio (in its own time): the harvest comes at the appointed time, not at the sower's preferred schedule; patience is essential to the agricultural metaphor.
10: ὡς καιρὸν ἔχομεν (as we have opportunity, G2540) — kairos is the appointed, opportune moment; the phrase acknowledges that opportunity is given, not perpetually available. τοὺς οἰκείους τῆς πίστεως (those of the household of faith) — the community of believers is named as the primary, though not exclusive, recipients of practical good.
11: ἴδετε πηλίκοις ὑμῖν γράμμασιν ἔγραψα τῇ ἐμῇ χειρί (see what large letters I write to you with my own hand) — Paul takes the pen from the scribe at this point (cf. 2 Thess 3:17; 1 Cor 16:21) to write a personal conclusion. The large letters may indicate poor eyesight (cf. 4:13-15), emphatic autograph, or both.
12: ὅσοι θέλουσιν εὐπροσωπῆσαι ἐν σαρκί (those who want to make a good showing in the flesh, G2146) — euprosōpeō (G2146, a hapax) means to appear well, put on a good face; the motivation is appearance management. μόνον ἵνα τῷ σταυρῷ τοῦ Χριστοῦ μὴ διώκωνται (only so that they might not be persecuted for the cross of Christ) — the ultimate motivation for the agitators is avoiding the social cost of the cross's scandal (5:11).
13: οὐδὲ γὰρ οἱ περιτεμνόμενοι αὐτοὶ νόμον φυλάσσουσιν (for not even those who are being circumcised keep the law themselves) — the present passive peritemnomenoi describes the ongoing circumcision-campaign; the exposure is ironic: the circumcision-advocates do not themselves keep the whole law they are requiring of others. ἀλλὰ θέλουσιν ὑμᾶς περιτέμνεσθαι, ἵνα ἐν τῇ ὑμετέρᾳ σαρκὶ καυχήσωνται (but they want you to be circumcised that they might boast in your flesh) — the flesh-boasting is the social credential of having brought Gentiles into circumcision.
14: ἐμοὶ δὲ μὴ γένοιτο καυχᾶσθαι εἰ μὴ ἐν τῷ σταυρῷ τοῦ κυρίου ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ (but far be it from me to boast except in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ, G4716) — the optative mē genoito is the strongest possible wish-rejection. The cross as the exclusive ground of boasting inverts the agitators' flesh-boasting. δι᾽ οὗ ἐμοὶ κόσμος ἐσταύρωται κἀγὼ κόσμῳ (through which the world has been crucified to me and I to the world) — both crucifixions are perfect passives: standing states; the world's claim on Paul and Paul's participation in the world's values have both been definitively ended by the cross.
15: οὔτε γὰρ περιτομή τί ἐστιν οὔτε ἀκροβυστία ἀλλὰ καινὴ κτίσις (for neither circumcision nor uncircumcision is anything but a new creation, G2537) — the two ethnic-religious markers are declared null in the sphere of the gospel (cf. 5:6; 3:28). Kainē ktisis (new creation) is the comprehensive eschatological category that replaces both: the new-creation work of Christ in the believer, not a minor religious modification.
16: τῷ κανόνι τούτῳ στοιχήσουσιν (as many as will march according to this rule, G2583) — kanōn (G2583) is the measuring standard or rule; the same stoicheō verb as 5:25 (walk in step with the Spirit). τὸν Ἰσραὴλ τοῦ θεοῦ (the Israel of God) — the phrase is debated: either all who follow the rule (Jew and Gentile = new covenant Israel) or specifically the Jewish believers who embrace the gospel. Given the letter's argument, it likely refers to all who are "in Christ" (3:29: "Abraham's seed"), which in Paul's usage constitutes the covenant community regardless of ethnicity.
17: τὰ στίγματα τοῦ Ἰησοῦ ἐν τῷ σώματί μου βαστάζω (I bear on my body the marks of Jesus, G4742) — stigmata (G4742) are brand marks indicating ownership; slaves and devotees were branded. Paul's stigmata are the bodily scars from beatings and persecution suffered for the gospel of Jesus (2 Cor 11:24-25). The claim answers the agitators: he does not need the circumcision mark (flesh-based) because he already bears Jesus's marks (suffering-based).
18: ἡ χάρις τοῦ κυρίου ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ μετὰ τοῦ πνεύματος ὑμῶν, ἀδελφοί (the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit, brothers) — the closing benediction invokes grace from the specific person of Jesus Christ; the Spirit (pneuma) here is the human spirit of the Galatians, not the Holy Spirit. The blessing of grace upon the spirit rather than merely on the body or external circumstances matches the letter's entire argument: the new covenant is in the spirit and by grace, not in the flesh and by works.
